items.title: investingLive Americas FX news wrap 14 Jul
items.summary: Crude oil settles at $79.34, up $1.20. Bias remains to the upside from a technical perspective Fitch affirms Canada at AA+ with a stable outlook Trump told Netanyahu that he should remove his soldiers from Lebanon Fed's Goolsbee: June CPI inflation data was surprisingly benign Trump: We will be doing a lot of deals with Iraq and taking a lot of oil out. Crude prices move lower after Trump backtracks on his 20% Reimbursement Fee Trump TACOs on the 20% toll in Hormuz Warsh Q&A: Inflation is a choice, we will deliver price stability RBC bumps up Canadian and US growth forecasts, sees Fed and BoC frozen through 2026 The USD is sharply lower after tame CPI inflation. What are the charts telling traders? Prepared Text from Fed Chair Warsh: Fed has no tolerance for persistent elevated inflation. US June CPI 3.5% vs 3.8% expected The USD is lower to start the NA session and ahead of the CPI and Warsh testimony. investingLive European FX news wrap: Markets consolidate ahead of the US CPI report The June U.S. CPI report came in well below expectations, offering the strongest sign in months that inflation pressures are easing after the spring rebound. Headline CPI slowed to 3.5% year-over-year from 4.2%, below the 3.8% forecast, while prices fell 0.4% on the month versus expectations for a 0.1% decline. Core inflation also surprised to the downside, easing to 2.6% year-over-year from 2.9%, while the monthly core reading was unchanged (0.0%), its softest pace since January 2021. The improvement was broad-based. Energy prices led the decline as gasoline prices tumbled, but underlying inflation also cooled, with shelter posting its smallest monthly increase since January 2021 and core services ex-shelter recording their weakest reading since May 2020. Softer readings in motor vehicle insurance, apparel, used cars, medical care, and lodging added to the encouraging report. The data prompted markets to scale back expectations for additional Fed tightening. The lower CPI sent the US dollar lower and ushered in the testimony on Capitol Hill by Fed Chair Warsh. Warsh delivered a measured but generally hawkish message, reiterating that the Fed's primary mission is to restore price stability and that "higher inflation is not acceptable." While he described the economy, labor market, and financial markets as solid, he emphasized that inflation remains the central challenge and that the Fed has the tools to bring it under control. Warsh avoided signaling the next move on interest rates, saying the FOMC will debate the timing and extent of any future policy actions and remain guided by the incoming data. Warsh also stressed the importance of Fed independence and greater transparency, while noting the central bank is looking beyond any single inflation gauge to better measure underlying price pressures. He added that quantitative easing is not inherently inflationary during periods of crisis and said he remains open-minded about balance-sheet reform. On today's CPI report, Warsh acknowledged that the data came in better than expected, but cautioned that one favorable inflation reading is not enough to change the Fed's outlook. He stressed that policymakers need to see a sustained improvement in inflation before considering any shift in policy, reinforcing that the fight against inflation is not yet over. During the testimony, President Trump announced that he is abandoning the proposed 20% U.S. reimbursement fee on ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz announced less than 24 hour ago, in favor of trade and investment agreements with Gulf States, saying the new approach will generate massive investment, expand factories and manufacturing, and create millions of high-paying American jobs. He credited the U.S. military and senior defense officials for keeping the Strait of Hormuz open to global shipping while maintaining a blockade on vessels traveling to or from Iranian ports or carrying Iranian cargo. Trump said the policy shift followed productive discussions with Middle Eastern leaders and argued that investment partnerships would deliver greater long-term benefits than the proposed fee. He also reiterated his hardline stance on Iran, insisting the country will never be allowed to obtain a nuclear weapon. As the dust settles, the USD is ending the session lower with the risk-on currencies (NZD, AUD, CAD) benefiting the most.  The NZD rose by 1.06% vs the greenback, the AUD by 0.81% and the CAD by 0.64%.  The CHF also benefitted by 0.63%.   The JPY was the smallest gainer with a gain of 0.13%.  Both the EUR and the GBP rose by 0.34% vs the USD.   The USD yields moved lower helping the dollars decline.  The shorter end of the yield curve fell the most with the 2-year yield falling by -7.6 basis points to 4.187%. That move took the yield down from a high of 4.298% earlier in the day - the highest level going back to February 19, but despite the decline, remains comfortably above the Fed target rate of 3.5% to 3.75%.  The 10 year yield fell -2.5 basis points to 4.585%.  US stocks closed higher led by the Nasdaq index which rose by 233 point or 0.90%. The Nasdaq also closed back above its 200 hour MA after testing the lower 100 hour MA yesterday and finding willing buyers.  If the price can stay above the 200 hour MA at 26025, the buyers remain in full control. A move below the 200 hour and 100 hour MA at 25873 would tilt the bias lower.  Crude oil prices rose $1.74 to $79.88, but is well off the high price at $81.27. Go prices rose by $52 and move back away from the $4000 level to $4053. Silver rose $1.11 or 1.95% to $58.75. Bitcoin caught a bid rising by $2321 to $64,471   This article was written by Greg Michalowski at investinglive.com.
items.summaryZh: 原油收于79.34美元，上涨1.20美元。从技术面看，走势仍偏向上行。惠誉确认加拿大评级为AA+，展望稳定。特朗普告诉内塔尼亚胡，他应该从黎巴嫩撤军。美联储的古尔斯比表示：6月份CPI通胀数据出人意料地温和。特朗普：我们将与伊拉克达成许多协议，并大量进口石油。特朗普就20%报销费政策改口后，原油价格走低 特朗普就霍尔木兹海峡20%过路费问题发表“塔可”式言论 沃什问答：通胀是一种选择，我们将实现物价稳定 加拿大皇家银行上调加拿大和美国经济增长预期，预计美联储和加拿大央行利率将维持不变至2026年 CPI通胀数据温和后，美元大幅走低。图表向交易员传递了什么信号？美联储主席沃什准备发言稿：美联储对持续高企的通胀零容忍。美国6月CPI为3.5%，低于预期值3.8%。北美交易时段伊始，在CPI数据及沃什证词公布前，美元走低。investingLive欧洲外汇新闻综述：市场在等待美国CPI报告前维持盘整。美国6月CPI报告远低于预期，这是数月来最强烈的信号，表明在春季反弹后，通胀压力正在缓解。 整体CPI同比增速从4.2%放缓至3.5%，低于3.8%的预期；而环比价格下降0.4%，而市场预期为下降0.1%。核心通胀也意外走低，同比增速从2.9%放缓至2.6%，而月度核心通胀率持平（0.0%），创下自2021年1月以来的最低增速。这一改善态势呈现全面态势。受汽油价格暴跌带动，能源价格领跌，但基础通胀也出现降温：住房价格录得2021年1月以来最小月度涨幅，剔除住房的核心服务价格则创下2020年5月以来的最低读数。机动车保险、服装、二手车、医疗保健和住宿等领域的通胀数据走软，进一步增强了这份报告的积极信号。这些数据促使市场下调了对美联储进一步收紧政策的预期。消费者物价指数（CPI）的回落导致美元走低，并为美联储主席沃什在国会山作证拉开了序幕。 沃什发表了一份措辞审慎但总体偏鹰派的讲话，重申美联储的首要任务是恢复物价稳定，并表示“更高的通胀是不可接受的”。尽管他将经济、劳动力市场和金融市场描述为稳健，但他强调通胀仍是核心挑战，且美联储拥有将通胀控制在可控范围内的工具。沃什避免就利率的下一步行动释放信号，表示联邦公开市场委员会（FOMC）将就未来任何政策行动的时机和幅度进行讨论，并继续以最新数据为指导。沃什还强调了美联储独立性和提高透明度的重要性，同时指出央行正着眼于单一通胀指标之外的因素，以更好地衡量潜在的价格压力。他补充说，在危机时期，量化宽松本身并不必然导致通胀，并表示他对资产负债表改革持开放态度。关于今日公布的CPI报告，沃什承认数据好于预期，但提醒道，仅凭一次利好的通胀读数尚不足以改变美联储的展望。 他强调，政策制定者需要看到通胀出现持续改善，才会考虑调整政策，并重申抗击通胀的斗争尚未结束。在作证期间，特朗普总统宣布，他将放弃不到24小时前提出的对经霍尔木兹海峡过境船舶征收20%美国费用补偿费的提议，转而支持与海湾国家达成贸易和投资协议。他表示，这一新举措将带来巨额投资，扩大工厂和制造业规模，并为美国创造数百万个高薪就业岗位。他赞扬美军和国防高层官员在维持对往返伊朗港口或运载伊朗货物的船舶封锁的同时，确保了霍尔木兹海峡对全球航运的畅通。特朗普表示，这一政策转变源于与中东领导人富有成效的讨论，并认为投资伙伴关系将比拟议的收费带来更大的长期效益。他还重申了对伊朗的强硬立场，坚称绝不允许该国获得核武器。 随着市场尘埃落定，美元收盘走低，风险偏好型货币（新西兰元、澳元、加元）受益最大。  新西兰元兑美元上涨1.06%，澳元上涨0.81%，加元上涨0.64%。  瑞士法郎也上涨了0.63%。    日元涨幅最小，仅上涨0.13%。欧元和英镑兑美元均上涨0.34%。  美元收益率走低，助推美元走弱。收益率曲线短端跌幅最大，2年期收益率下跌7.6个基点，至4.187%。这一走势使收益率从当日早些时候的4.298%高点回落——该水平是自2月19日以来的最高点，但尽管有所回落，仍远高于美联储3.5%至3.75%的目标利率区间。10年期收益率下跌2.5个基点，至4.585%。受纳斯达克指数领涨带动，美股收高，该指数上涨233点，涨幅0.90%。纳斯达克指数昨日触及100小时移动平均线下方后，因买盘积极支撑，收盘时重新站上200小时移动平均线。若价格能维持在26025点的200小时移动平均线上方，买方将持续占据主导地位。 若跌破位于25873点的200小时和100小时移动平均线，市场走势将转为看跌。 原油价格上涨1.74美元至79.88美元，但 仍远低于81.27美元的高点。黄金价格上涨52美元，从4000美元关口回落至4053美元。白银上涨1.11美元（涨幅1.95%），报58.75美元。比特币获得买盘支撑，上涨2321美元，报64,471美元。本文作者为investinglive.com的格雷格·米哈洛夫斯基。

items.title: investingLive Asia-pacific FX news wrap 14 Jul
items.summary: China June trade data smashes forecasts on AI demand surge NZD firms as Conway reinforces RBNZ hawkish tilt on inflation risk China's export growth accelerated in June, surged 27% y/y vs. 18.2% expected Preview - Falling gas prices seen pulling US June CPI lower, but Fed unlikely to relax Gold falls to two week low as oil surge stokes Fed hike bets Australian business mood improves in June, but survey misses new oil spike ICYMI - European Union imposes gold sanctions / ban Australia Business Confidence June 2026: -5 (vs. prior -14) PBOC sets USD/ CNY central rate at 6.7990 (vs. estimate at 6.7927) Japan finance minister comments giving the yen a wee boost RBA rate hikes still weighing even as confidence ticks higher Bank of Korea seen hiking to 2.75% this week, on Thursday, more to come by year end Recap - RBNZ warns oil driven inflation could become persistent, more hikes eyed Singapore economy cools from Q1 pace as MAS policy call nears Yen Katayama bounce fully unwinds as GPIF shift doubts emerge UK consumers spend on heat and football even as risks build ICYMI - Iranian missiles hit two UAE tankers in Hormuz, killing one crew member RBNZ Chief Economist Conway, more: "Will get inflation back to 2%" Tanker hit by projectile off Oman coast hours before Hormuz blockade RBNZ signals further tightening as conflict lifts inflation risk NZ QSBO business confidence rebounds but rising cost pressures cloud RBNZ path ICYMI - UAE plans new Fujairah port to bypass Strait of Hormuz, FT reports US military says it has begun a third consecutive night of strikes against Iran Recap - Crude surges as Saudi Arabia comes under attack and Iran blockade returns NASDAQ index leads the stocks to the downside Summary: Oil extended gains after Monday's 9% plus surge, with the US military carrying out a third consecutive night of strikes on Iran and the naval blockade of Iranian shipping now in force Gold briefly slipped under 4,000 dollars as the inflation and rate hike implications of the oil spike outweighed any safe haven bid The New Zealand dollar moved higher on RBNZ chief economist Conway's comments reinforcing the case for further tightening if Middle East linked inflation proves persistent The yen rallied briefly after Japan's Finance Minister Katayama reopened the door to a possible GPIF allocation shift, without committing to anything concrete China's June trade data smashed forecasts, with exports growing at their fastest pace since 2021 and imports at a five year high South Korean shares fell again, triggering a five minute sidecar halt in program trading after the KOSDAQ dropped 5% Oil extended its gains on Tuesday after Monday's rally of more than 9%, as the US military carried out a third consecutive night of strikes against Iran and the reinstated naval blockade of Iranian shipping took hold. The escalation has now touched multiple fronts within a single session, from direct attacks on Saudi soil to a fatal Iranian missile strike on two UAE tankers in the southern Strait of Hormuz, and Trump's warning of a possible strike on a hardened nuclear facility near Natanz has added a further layer of risk rather than any sign of de-escalation. That combination has left the market pricing a conflict showing no obvious path toward containment, and it is the backdrop against which nearly every other story in today's session needs to be read. Gold, typically a beneficiary of exactly this kind of geopolitical stress, instead briefly slipped under 4,000 dollars, a reminder that the inflation and Fed rate hike implications of higher oil are currently outweighing any safe haven demand for the metal. The New Zealand dollar was a standout mover, helped by RBNZ chief economist Conway reinforcing the central bank's hawkish framing. Conway confirmed last week's 25 basis point hike to 2.5%, described it as a calibrated drift back toward neutral rather than a shift to restrictive policy, and explicitly said the RBNZ will respond further if Middle East linked inflation pressures prove more persistent than expected, a fairly direct piece of forward guidance that gave the currency a clean fundamental tailwind. The yen also found some support after Japan's Finance Minister Katayama said a change to GPIF's asset allocation could be examined if the investment environment shifts sharply, days after a report suggested no immediate change was planned. The remarks gave markets a reason to keep pricing some probability of repatriation without offering anything concrete enough to reignite last week's scale of buying, and comments on wanting to enhance the appeal of Japanese government bonds suggest Tokyo is keen to keep the broader asset attractiveness story alive on multiple fronts rather than resting on the GPIF question alone. The yen's rally proved brief, consistent with a market now treating this kind of comment with more caution after last week's round trip. China provided the session's clearest upside surprise, with June trade data beating forecasts comprehensively. Exports rose 27% year on year, the fastest pace since 2021, while imports jumped 36% to a five year high, both far ahead of consensus as AI related demand and a rush of shipments ahead of possible US tariffs supported activity. The data raises the stakes for Wednesday's second quarter GDP release. South Korean equities were the session's weak spot, falling again and forcing the Korea Exchange to activate its sidecar mechanism, halting program trading for five minutes after the KOSDAQ dropped 5%, a further sign of how unsettled regional risk appetite has become as the conflict grinds on. --- Still to come Tuesday are June CPI data from the US. There's a preview linked in the bullets above and here is an earlier one from Goldman Sachs. This table of forecasts is via the Wall Street Journal: This article was written by Eamonn Sheridan at investinglive.com.
items.summaryZh: 中国6月贸易数据远超预期，得益于AI需求激增；康威重申新西兰联储对通胀风险的鹰派立场，纽元走强；中国6月出口增速加快，同比大增27%，远超预期18.2% 预览 - 天然气价格下跌预计将拉低美国6月CPI，但美联储不太可能放松政策 受油价飙升推高美联储加息预期影响，金价跌至两周低点 澳大利亚6月商业信心改善，但调查未反映出新一轮油价飙升 值得关注 - 欧盟实施黄金制裁/禁令 澳大利亚2026年6月商业信心指数： -5（前值-14） 中国人民银行将美元兑人民币中间价定为6.7990（前值 （预计为6.7927）日本财务大臣的言论使日元小幅走强；尽管信心指数小幅上升，但澳大利亚储备银行的加息仍构成压力；预计韩国央行将于本周四将利率上调至2.75%，年底前还将进一步加息。回顾 - 新西兰央行警告称，由油价推动的通胀可能持续，预计将进一步加息；新加坡经济增速较第一季度放缓，新加坡金融管理局即将公布政策决议；随着日本政府养老金投资基金（GPIF）调整策略引发疑虑，片山日元反弹行情完全回吐；英国消费者在风险加剧之际仍将开支用于取暖和足球；【错过了吗】——伊朗导弹在霍尔木兹海峡击中两艘阿联酋油轮，造成一名船员死亡；新西兰央行首席经济学家康韦等： “将把通胀率降回2%” 霍尔木兹海峡封锁前数小时，一艘油轮在阿曼近海遭弹道物体击中 随着冲突加剧通胀风险，新西兰联储释放进一步收紧政策的信号 新西兰QSBO商业信心指数反弹，但成本压力上升给新西兰联储的政策路径蒙上阴影 您可能错过了——据《金融时报》报道，阿联酋计划建设新的富查伊拉港以绕过霍尔木兹海峡 美军称已连续第三个夜晚对伊朗发动空袭 回顾 - 沙特遭袭且伊朗封锁重启，原油价格飙升 纳斯达克指数领跌股市 摘要：周一暴涨逾9%后，油价延续涨势，随着 美军连续第三个夜晚对伊朗发动空袭，对伊朗船只的海上封锁现已生效。受油价飙升引发的通胀和加息预期影响，黄金价格一度跌破4,000美元，这一影响盖过了避险买盘。新西兰元走高，因新西兰储备银行首席经济学家康韦的 的言论推动下走高，其强调若中东相关通胀持续存在，将进一步收紧货币政策。日本财务大臣片山重启了政府养老金投资基金（GPIF）调整资产配置的可能性，但未作出任何具体承诺，日元随后短暂走强。中国6月贸易数据远超预期，出口增速创2021年以来新高，进口增速达五年最高水平。韩国股市再度下跌， 因KOSDAQ指数下跌5%，触发了程序化交易五分钟的“侧车”暂停机制。周二，油价延续了周一逾9%的涨幅，此前美军连续第三个夜晚对伊朗发动空袭，且针对伊朗船只的海上封锁措施重新生效并开始实施。 局势升级如今已在单个交易日内波及多个方面，从沙特境内遭遇的直接袭击，到伊朗在霍尔木兹海峡南部对两艘阿联酋油轮发动的致命导弹袭击，再到特朗普警告可能对纳坦兹附近一座加固的核设施发动打击——这些事件非但未显现任何缓和迹象，反而进一步加剧了风险。这一系列事件使得市场定价反映出冲突似乎没有明显的缓和途径，而这也是解读今日交易时段中几乎所有其他事件的背景。黄金通常正是此类地缘政治紧张局势的受益者，但此次却短暂跌破4,000美元大关，这提醒人们，油价上涨带来的通胀压力及美联储加息预期，目前正压倒市场对黄金的避险需求。新西兰元走势尤为突出，这得益于新西兰联储首席经济学家康韦重申了该行鹰派立场。 康威确认了上周将利率上调25个基点至2.5%的决定，将其描述为“有节制地回归中性政策”，而非转向紧缩政策，并明确表示，如果与中东相关的通胀压力比预期更为持久，新西兰央行将采取进一步行动。这一相当直接的前瞻性指引为新西兰元提供了明确的基本面利好。此前有报道称日本政府养老金投资基金（GPIF）暂无立即调整资产配置的计划，但数日后，日本财务大臣片山表示，若投资环境发生剧变，将考虑调整GPIF的资产配置，这为日元提供了部分支撑。这些言论让市场有理由继续定价一定概率的资金回流，但并未提供足够具体的依据来重燃上周那样的买盘规模；而关于希望提升日本国债吸引力的评论则表明，东京方面希望在多个层面维持日本资产的整体吸引力，而非仅依赖GPIF这一因素。日元涨势最终短暂，这与市场在经历上周的大起大落后，如今对这类评论持更加谨慎态度的现状相符。 中国为本交易日带来了最明显的积极意外，6月贸易数据全面超出预期。出口同比增长27%，创下2021年以来最快增速；进口则飙升36%，达到五年高点。这两项数据均远超市场共识，主要得益于人工智能相关需求以及为规避美国可能加征的关税而进行的抢运。这些数据也使得周三即将公布的第二季度GDP数据备受关注。韩国股市是本交易日表现最疲软的板块，再次下跌，迫使韩国交易所启动“侧车”机制——在KOSDAQ指数下跌5%后，暂停程序化交易五分钟。随着冲突持续，这进一步表明该地区风险偏好已变得多么动荡。--- 周二还将公布美国6月CPI数据。上文要点中已附有相关预览链接，此处另附高盛此前发布的预览报告。下表中的预测数据来自《华尔街日报》：本文作者为investinglive.com的Eamonn Sheridan。
items.summaryEn: China June trade data smashes forecasts on AI demand surge NZD firms as Conway reinforces RBNZ hawkish tilt on inflation risk China's export growth accelerated in June, surged 27% y/y vs. 18.2% expected Preview - Falling gas prices seen pulling US June CPI lower, but Fed unlikely to relax Gold falls to two week low as oil surge stokes Fed hike bets Australian business mood improves in June, but survey misses new oil spike ICYMI - European Union imposes gold sanctions / ban Australia Business Confidence June 2026: -5 (vs. prior -14) PBOC sets USD/ CNY central rate at 6.7990 (vs. estimate at 6.7927) Japan finance minister comments giving the yen a wee boost RBA rate hikes still weighing even as confidence ticks higher Bank of Korea seen hiking to 2.75% this week, on Thursday, more to come by year end Recap - RBNZ warns oil driven inflation could become persistent, more hikes eyed Singapore economy cools from Q1 pace as MAS policy call nears Yen Katayama bounce fully unwinds as GPIF shift doubts emerge UK consumers spend on heat and football even as risks build ICYMI - Iranian missiles hit two UAE tankers in Hormuz, killing one crew member RBNZ Chief Economist Conway, more: "Will get inflation back to 2%" Tanker hit by projectile off Oman coast hours before Hormuz blockade RBNZ signals further tightening as conflict lifts inflation risk NZ QSBO business confidence rebounds but rising cost pressures cloud RBNZ path ICYMI - UAE plans new Fujairah port to bypass Strait of Hormuz, FT reports US military says it has begun a third consecutive night of strikes against Iran Recap - Crude surges as Saudi Arabia comes under attack and Iran blockade returns NASDAQ index leads the stocks to the downside Summary: Oil extended gains after Monday's 9% plus surge, with the US military carrying out a third consecutive night of strikes on Iran and the naval blockade of Iranian shipping now in force Gold briefly slipped under 4,000 dollars as the inflation and rate hike implications of the oil spike outweighed any safe haven bid The New Zealand dollar moved higher on RBNZ chief economist Conway's comments reinforcing the case for further tightening if Middle East linked inflation proves persistent The yen rallied briefly after Japan's Finance Minister Katayama reopened the door to a possible GPIF allocation shift, without committing to anything concrete China's June trade data smashed forecasts, with exports growing at their fastest pace since 2021 and imports at a five year high South Korean shares fell again, triggering a five minute sidecar halt in program trading after the KOSDAQ dropped 5% Oil extended its gains on Tuesday after Monday's rally of more than 9%, as the US military carried out a third consecutive night of strikes against Iran and the reinstated naval blockade of Iranian shipping took hold. The escalation has now touched multiple fronts within a single session, from direct attacks on Saudi soil to a fatal Iranian missile strike on two UAE tankers in the southern Strait of Hormuz, and Trump's warning of a possible strike on a hardened nuclear facility near Natanz has added a further layer of risk rather than any sign of de-escalation. That combination has left the market pricing a conflict showing no obvious path toward containment, and it is the backdrop against which nearly every other story in today's session needs to be read. Gold, typically a beneficiary of exactly this kind of geopolitical stress, instead briefly slipped under 4,000 dollars, a reminder that the inflation and Fed rate hike implications of higher oil are currently outweighing any safe haven demand for the metal. The New Zealand dollar was a standout mover, helped by RBNZ chief economist Conway reinforcing the central bank's hawkish framing. Conway confirmed last week's 25 basis point hike to 2.5%, described it as a calibrated drift back toward neutral rather than a shift to restrictive policy, and explicitly said the RBNZ will respond further if Middle East linked inflation pressures prove more persistent than expected, a fairly direct piece of forward guidance that gave the currency a clean fundamental tailwind. The yen also found some support after Japan's Finance Minister Katayama said a change to GPIF's asset allocation could be examined if the investment environment shifts sharply, days after a report suggested no immediate change was planned. The remarks gave markets a reason to keep pricing some probability of repatriation without offering anything concrete enough to reignite last week's scale of buying, and comments on wanting to enhance the appeal of Japanese government bonds suggest Tokyo is keen to keep the broader asset attractiveness story alive on multiple fronts rather than resting on the GPIF question alone. The yen's rally proved brief, consistent with a market now treating this kind of comment with more caution after last week's round trip. China provided the session's clearest upside surprise, with June trade data beating forecasts comprehensively. Exports rose 27% year on year, the fastest pace since 2021, while imports jumped 36% to a five year high, both far ahead of consensus as AI related demand and a rush of shipments ahead of possible US tariffs supported activity. The data raises the stakes for Wednesday's second quarter GDP release. South Korean equities were the session's weak spot, falling again and forcing the Korea Exchange to activate its sidecar mechanism, halting program trading for five minutes after the KOSDAQ dropped 5%, a further sign of how unsettled regional risk appetite has become as the conflict grinds on. --- Still to come Tuesday are June CPI data from the US. There's a preview linked in the bullets above and here is an earlier one from Goldman Sachs. This table of forecasts is via the Wall Street Journal: This article was written by Eamonn Sheridan at investinglive.com.

items.title: investingLive Asia-pacific FX news wrap 15 Jul
items.summary: Nikkei rises but gains checked ahead of ASML earnings, Kospi surges China Q2 GDP growth cools to 4.3%, weakest pace in three and a half years US forces hit dozens of Iranian military sites near Strait of Hormuz China June Retail sales +1.0% y/y (expected -0.1%) and Industrial output +5.3% y/y (expected 4.6%) China Q2 GDP 0.9% q/q (expected 0.9%) and 4.3% y/y (expected 4.5%) New home prices in China fell for a fourth straight year in June China House prices June 2026 -3.3% y/y (prior -3.5%) Oil escalation - Iran hit US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan Korea Exchange halted trading twice as Kospi and Kosdaq futures rocketed. SK Hynix +12% PBOC sets USD/ CNY mid-point today at 6.7910 (vs. estimate at 6.7965) Gulf states race to pipeline oil around Hormuz, Goldman Sachs finds. Could insulate most Gulf oil exports. New Zealand retail data, June 2026: +1.3% y/y (vs. prior +3.3%) Japan - Reuters Tankan shows split business mood as Middle East risks linger Here's the key test for next Bank of Japan (BoJ) rate hike: Whether price rises stick Trump confirms US held talks with Iran, urged Tehran to reach a deal ICYMI - China targets 60tn yuan (~US$8.84tn) retail sales by 2030 under new consumption plan Heads up for Lucid funded traders, the talk about Lucid bankruptcy is a completely different firm Recapping a crude Tuesday: Oil prices climb to one-month high as Middle East conflict escalates Private survey inventory shows a headline crude oil draw smaller than expected Summary: Trump told Fox News strikes on Iran will continue until he deems it enough, confirmed the US held talks with Tehran and urged a deal, while flagging energy targets are being saved for later in the campaign Axios reported Trump convened a Situation Room meeting on Tuesday to weigh a larger new wave of strikes on Iran, with Trump having already floated hitting more sensitive sites including bridges and infrastructure Iran struck US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan overnight in retaliation Centcom said it completed a fresh seven-hour wave of strikes on dozens of Iranian targets near the Strait of Hormuz, coinciding with the resumed naval blockade of Iranian ports from 4pm ET Net oil price impact from the latest escalation has been comparatively minor so far, while the US dollar lost further ground Japan's Reuters Tankan held at +13 for manufacturers in July, unchanged from June, while the non-manufacturers' index eased to +25 from +32; both are seen holding into October, with the data unlikely to shift the BOJ's hawkish bias, though markets still see little chance of a hike this month, more likely September or October Japan's May core machinery orders fell 12.4% month-on-month, far below the 4.2% decline expected and reversing an 8.7% rise in April, with the year-on-year reading also missing forecasts; the data is dated and volatile, and likely reflects payback from April's strength given manufacturers' otherwise upbeat mood China's Q2 GDP grew 4.3% year-on-year, the weakest since Q4 2022 and below forecasts, as June retail sales and industrial output beat expectations but property investment and broader fixed-asset investment continued to slump, alongside a further, if slightly slower, decline in June new home prices Asian equities rallied hard on a softer-than-expected US inflation print that cooled July Fed hike bets, with Korea's Kospi surging 6.9%, triggering sidecar halts on both the Kospi and Kosdaq as SK Hynix jumped nearly 12%, while Japan's Nikkei and Topix, and Australian shares, also posted gains It was a session defined by two parallel tracks, an escalating US-Iran conflict running alongside a broad, inflation-driven risk rally across Asian equities, with the two dynamics so far coexisting rather than colliding. On the geopolitical side, President Trump told Fox News in an interview aired Tuesday night that strikes on Iran would continue until he personally judged the campaign had done enough, adding that Tehran retained some fight but not much, and that energy targets were being deliberately held back for later in the campaign. He also confirmed that the US had held talks with Iran on Tuesday and had urged Tehran to reach a deal, keeping a diplomatic track alive alongside the military one. That picture of restraint sat uneasily against a separate Axios report that Trump convened a Situation Room meeting on Tuesday to weigh a larger new wave of strikes on Iran, with Trump having already talked publicly about potentially hitting more sensitive targets, including bridges and broader infrastructure. Iran responded with strikes on US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan overnight, widening the conflict's reach across the region. Into the Asian session, Centcom confirmed it had completed an additional, seven-hour wave of strikes on dozens of Iranian military targets near the Strait of Hormuz, timed alongside the resumption of the US naval blockade of Iranian ports and coastal areas from 4pm ET. Despite the scale of the latest escalation, the net impact on oil prices has so far been comparatively minor, while the US dollar has lost further ground. In Japan, the data flow was mixed. The Reuters Tankan showed manufacturers' sentiment steady at +13 in July, unchanged from June and supported by strong semiconductor and AI server demand, while non-manufacturers' sentiment eased to +25 from +32 on cost pressures tied to the Middle East conflict and a weak yen. Both indexes are expected to hold into October. Separately, May core machinery orders fell 12.4% month-on-month, well short of the 4.2% decline expected and a sharp reversal from April's 8.7% gain, with the year-on-year figure of -1.9% also missing forecasts for a 12.9% rise. The series is notoriously volatile and the data itself is dated, and the drop looks largely like payback from April's strength rather than a genuine turn, particularly given how upbeat manufacturers otherwise remain. None of this is expected to shift the BOJ's hawkish stance, with markets still assigning little chance of a hike this month but placing better odds on September or October. China's data dump added another layer. Second-quarter GDP grew 4.3% year-on-year, missing forecasts and marking the weakest pace since the pandemic-hit final quarter of 2022, as the property slump and oil-shock fallout offset stronger factory output and exports. June activity data was more encouraging on the consumption side, with retail sales up 1.0% and industrial output up 5.3%, both beating expectations, but fixed-asset investment and property investment remained deeply negative, and new home prices fell again in June, albeit at a marginally slower pace than in May. Equity markets, meanwhile, were in full risk-on mode after a softer-than-expected US inflation report cooled expectations for a July Fed hike and extended an overnight Wall Street rally built on strong bank earnings and chipmaker strength. South Korea's Kospi was the standout, surging 6.9% and triggering sidecar halts on both the Kospi and Kosdaq as futures spiked, with SK Hynix jumping nearly 12% in tandem with the overnight US tech rally. Japan's Nikkei and Topix also advanced, alongside Australian shares, though the Nikkei's gains were tempered somewhat by caution ahead of ASML's earnings later in the day, a reminder that the AI chip rally's durability still depends on confirmation from the sector's key suppliers. This article was written by Eamonn Sheridan at investinglive.com.
items.summaryZh: 日经指数上涨，但在ASML财报公布前涨幅受限；韩国KOSPI指数大涨；中国二季度GDP增速放缓至4.3%，为三年半来最低水平；美军袭击了霍尔木兹海峡附近数十处伊朗军事设施；中国6月零售额同比增长1.0%（预期-0.1%），工业产出同比增长5.3%（预期4.6%） 中国二季度GDP环比增长0.9%（预期0.9%），同比增长4.3%（预期4.5%） 中国6月新房价格连续第四年下跌；中国2026年6月房价同比下降3.3%（前值-3.5%）；石油局势升级——伊朗袭击了美国在巴林、科威特和约旦的军事基地；韩国交易所因KOSPI和KOSDAQ期货价格暴涨而两度暂停交易。SK海力士股价上涨12%。中国人民银行今日将美元兑人民币中间价定为6.7910（市场预期为6.7965）。高盛研究显示，海湾国家正竞相在霍尔木兹海峡周边铺设输油管道，此举或可保障大部分海湾地区石油出口。新西兰2026年6月零售数据：同比上涨1.3%（相比 较前值上涨3.3%）日本——路透社：随着中东风险持续，日本“短观”调查显示企业情绪分化 这是日本央行（BoJ）下次加息的关键考验： 物价上涨能否持续 特朗普证实美国曾与伊朗进行磋商，敦促德黑兰达成协议 错过了吗？——中国新消费计划设定2030年零售额目标为60万亿元（约合8.84万亿美元） 提醒Lucid资助的交易员注意：关于Lucid破产的传言涉及的是另一家公司 回顾“原油星期二”： 随着中东冲突升级，油价攀升至一个月高点 私营调查数据显示，原油库存降幅小于预期 总结： 特朗普告诉福克斯新闻，对伊朗的打击将持续到他认为足够为止；他证实美国曾与德黑兰进行过谈判并敦促达成协议，同时表示能源目标将留待竞选后期再谈；Axios报道称，特朗普周二召集了一次情况室会议，以权衡对伊朗发动新一轮更大规模打击的可能性，此前特朗普已提出打击更多敏感目标，包括桥梁和基础设施；伊朗袭击了巴林、 科威特和约旦的美国基地。中央司令部表示，已完成新一轮持续七小时的空袭，打击了数十个伊朗目标 当船只接近霍尔木兹海峡时，恰逢美国东部时间下午4点起对伊朗港口的海上封锁重新开始。迄今为止，最新局势升级对油价的净影响相对较小，而美元进一步走弱。日本路透社7月“短观”调查显示，制造业指数维持在+13，与6月持平；而非制造业指数则从+32小幅回落至+25； 预计这两项指数将维持至10月，该数据不太可能改变日本央行（BOJ）的鹰派倾向，尽管市场仍认为本月加息的可能性很小，更可能在9月或10月；日本5月核心机械订单环比下降12.4%，远低于预期降幅4.2%，扭转了4月8.7%的增幅，同比数据也未达预期； 该数据滞后且波动较大，鉴于制造商整体情绪乐观，这很可能反映了4月份强劲表现后的回调；中国第二季度GDP同比增长4.3%，为2022年第四季度以来最低水平，且低于预期；尽管6月份零售额和工业产出好于预期，但房地产投资和更广泛的固定资产投资持续低迷，同时， 尽管降幅略有收窄，6月新房价格仍继续下滑；亚洲股市在 美国通胀数据低于预期，这使得市场对美联储7月加息的预期降温；韩国KOSPI指数飙升6.9%，由于SK海力士股价大涨近12%，KOSPI和KOSDAQ两大指数均触发“侧车”停牌机制；与此同时，日本日经指数和东证指数， 以及澳大利亚股市也均录得涨幅。本交易日呈现双轨并行的格局：美伊冲突不断升级，与亚洲股市因通胀预期推动而出现的广泛风险偏好反弹并行发展，目前这两种动态尚处于共存状态，而非相互冲突。在地缘政治方面，特朗普总统在周二晚间播出的福克斯新闻采访中表示，对伊朗的打击将持续进行，直到他本人认为行动已取得足够成效为止。他补充说，德黑兰仍保有一定战斗力，但已所剩无几，且能源目标正被有意保留，以备行动后期使用。他还证实，美国周二已与伊朗举行了会谈，并敦促德黑兰达成协议，在军事行动的同时保持外交渠道畅通。 这种克制的姿态与Axios的另一则报道形成鲜明对比：据报道，特朗普周二召集了情况室会议，商讨对伊朗发动新一轮更大规模的空袭，此前特朗普已公开表示可能打击更多敏感目标，包括桥梁和更广泛的基础设施。伊朗随即在当晚对巴林、科威特和约旦境内的美军基地发动了袭击，使冲突范围进一步扩大至整个地区。进入亚洲交易时段，美国中央司令部证实，已对霍尔木兹海峡附近的数十个伊朗军事目标完成了新一轮为期七小时的空袭，此次行动与美国自美东时间下午4点起恢复对伊朗港口及沿海地区的海上封锁同步进行。尽管此次局势升级规模较大，但迄今为止对油价的净影响相对较小，而美元则进一步走弱。在日本，经济数据喜忧参半。路透社短观调查显示，7月制造业景气指数维持在+13，与6月持平，这得益于半导体和AI服务器需求的强劲；而非制造业景气指数则从+32降至+25，主要受中东冲突引发的成本压力及日元走软的影响。 预计这两项指数将在10月份保持稳定。此外，5月份核心机械订单环比下降12.4%，远低于预期降幅4.2%，与4月份8.7%的增幅形成急剧反转；同比数据为-1.9%，也未达到预期12.9%的增幅。该数据系列素以波动剧烈著称，且数据本身已过时，此次下滑在很大程度上似乎是4月强劲表现后的回调，而非真正的转折，特别是考虑到制造业整体仍保持乐观态势。预计这些因素均不会改变日本央行（BOJ）的鹰派立场，市场仍认为本月加息的可能性极低，但对9月或10月加息的预期更高。中国发布的一系列数据又增添了新的变数。第二季度GDP同比增长4.3%，低于预期，创下自2022年受疫情冲击的第四季度以来的最低增速，原因是房地产市场低迷和石油冲击的余波抵消了工业产出和出口的强劲表现。 6月份的经济数据在消费方面更为令人鼓舞，零售额增长1.0%，工业产出增长5.3%，均超出预期；但固定资产投资和房地产投资仍深陷负增长，6月份新房价格再次下跌，尽管跌幅较5月份略有收窄。与此同时，在低于预期的美国通胀报告降温了市场对美联储7月加息的预期后，股市全面进入风险偏好模式，并延续了前一晚基于银行强劲财报和芯片制造商表现强劲而引发的华尔街涨势。韩国KOSPI指数表现尤为抢眼，飙升6.9%，并因期货价格暴涨触发了KOSPI和KOSDAQ两指数的“侧车”停盘机制，其中SK海力士受隔夜美国科技股上涨带动，股价大涨近12%。日本日经指数和东证指数也随澳大利亚股市一同上涨，不过由于市场对当天晚些时候ASML将公布的财报持谨慎态度，日经指数的涨幅有所收窄，这提醒人们，AI芯片行情的可持续性仍取决于该行业关键供应商的业绩表现。本文作者为investinglive.com的Eamonn Sheridan。
items.summaryEn: Nikkei rises but gains checked ahead of ASML earnings, Kospi surges China Q2 GDP growth cools to 4.3%, weakest pace in three and a half years US forces hit dozens of Iranian military sites near Strait of Hormuz China June Retail sales +1.0% y/y (expected -0.1%) and Industrial output +5.3% y/y (expected 4.6%) China Q2 GDP 0.9% q/q (expected 0.9%) and 4.3% y/y (expected 4.5%) New home prices in China fell for a fourth straight year in June China House prices June 2026 -3.3% y/y (prior -3.5%) Oil escalation - Iran hit US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan Korea Exchange halted trading twice as Kospi and Kosdaq futures rocketed. SK Hynix +12% PBOC sets USD/ CNY mid-point today at 6.7910 (vs. estimate at 6.7965) Gulf states race to pipeline oil around Hormuz, Goldman Sachs finds. Could insulate most Gulf oil exports. New Zealand retail data, June 2026: +1.3% y/y (vs. prior +3.3%) Japan - Reuters Tankan shows split business mood as Middle East risks linger Here's the key test for next Bank of Japan (BoJ) rate hike: Whether price rises stick Trump confirms US held talks with Iran, urged Tehran to reach a deal ICYMI - China targets 60tn yuan (~US$8.84tn) retail sales by 2030 under new consumption plan Heads up for Lucid funded traders, the talk about Lucid bankruptcy is a completely different firm Recapping a crude Tuesday: Oil prices climb to one-month high as Middle East conflict escalates Private survey inventory shows a headline crude oil draw smaller than expected Summary: Trump told Fox News strikes on Iran will continue until he deems it enough, confirmed the US held talks with Tehran and urged a deal, while flagging energy targets are being saved for later in the campaign Axios reported Trump convened a Situation Room meeting on Tuesday to weigh a larger new wave of strikes on Iran, with Trump having already floated hitting more sensitive sites including bridges and infrastructure Iran struck US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan overnight in retaliation Centcom said it completed a fresh seven-hour wave of strikes on dozens of Iranian targets near the Strait of Hormuz, coinciding with the resumed naval blockade of Iranian ports from 4pm ET Net oil price impact from the latest escalation has been comparatively minor so far, while the US dollar lost further ground Japan's Reuters Tankan held at +13 for manufacturers in July, unchanged from June, while the non-manufacturers' index eased to +25 from +32; both are seen holding into October, with the data unlikely to shift the BOJ's hawkish bias, though markets still see little chance of a hike this month, more likely September or October Japan's May core machinery orders fell 12.4% month-on-month, far below the 4.2% decline expected and reversing an 8.7% rise in April, with the year-on-year reading also missing forecasts; the data is dated and volatile, and likely reflects payback from April's strength given manufacturers' otherwise upbeat mood China's Q2 GDP grew 4.3% year-on-year, the weakest since Q4 2022 and below forecasts, as June retail sales and industrial output beat expectations but property investment and broader fixed-asset investment continued to slump, alongside a further, if slightly slower, decline in June new home prices Asian equities rallied hard on a softer-than-expected US inflation print that cooled July Fed hike bets, with Korea's Kospi surging 6.9%, triggering sidecar halts on both the Kospi and Kosdaq as SK Hynix jumped nearly 12%, while Japan's Nikkei and Topix, and Australian shares, also posted gains It was a session defined by two parallel tracks, an escalating US-Iran conflict running alongside a broad, inflation-driven risk rally across Asian equities, with the two dynamics so far coexisting rather than colliding. On the geopolitical side, President Trump told Fox News in an interview aired Tuesday night that strikes on Iran would continue until he personally judged the campaign had done enough, adding that Tehran retained some fight but not much, and that energy targets were being deliberately held back for later in the campaign. He also confirmed that the US had held talks with Iran on Tuesday and had urged Tehran to reach a deal, keeping a diplomatic track alive alongside the military one. That picture of restraint sat uneasily against a separate Axios report that Trump convened a Situation Room meeting on Tuesday to weigh a larger new wave of strikes on Iran, with Trump having already talked publicly about potentially hitting more sensitive targets, including bridges and broader infrastructure. Iran responded with strikes on US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan overnight, widening the conflict's reach across the region. Into the Asian session, Centcom confirmed it had completed an additional, seven-hour wave of strikes on dozens of Iranian military targets near the Strait of Hormuz, timed alongside the resumption of the US naval blockade of Iranian ports and coastal areas from 4pm ET. Despite the scale of the latest escalation, the net impact on oil prices has so far been comparatively minor, while the US dollar has lost further ground. In Japan, the data flow was mixed. The Reuters Tankan showed manufacturers' sentiment steady at +13 in July, unchanged from June and supported by strong semiconductor and AI server demand, while non-manufacturers' sentiment eased to +25 from +32 on cost pressures tied to the Middle East conflict and a weak yen. Both indexes are expected to hold into October. Separately, May core machinery orders fell 12.4% month-on-month, well short of the 4.2% decline expected and a sharp reversal from April's 8.7% gain, with the year-on-year figure of -1.9% also missing forecasts for a 12.9% rise. The series is notoriously volatile and the data itself is dated, and the drop looks largely like payback from April's strength rather than a genuine turn, particularly given how upbeat manufacturers otherwise remain. None of this is expected to shift the BOJ's hawkish stance, with markets still assigning little chance of a hike this month but placing better odds on September or October. China's data dump added another layer. Second-quarter GDP grew 4.3% year-on-year, missing forecasts and marking the weakest pace since the pandemic-hit final quarter of 2022, as the property slump and oil-shock fallout offset stronger factory output and exports. June activity data was more encouraging on the consumption side, with retail sales up 1.0% and industrial output up 5.3%, both beating expectations, but fixed-asset investment and property investment remained deeply negative, and new home prices fell again in June, albeit at a marginally slower pace than in May. Equity markets, meanwhile, were in full risk-on mode after a softer-than-expected US inflation report cooled expectations for a July Fed hike and extended an overnight Wall Street rally built on strong bank earnings and chipmaker strength. South Korea's Kospi was the standout, surging 6.9% and triggering sidecar halts on both the Kospi and Kosdaq as futures spiked, with SK Hynix jumping nearly 12% in tandem with the overnight US tech rally. Japan's Nikkei and Topix also advanced, alongside Australian shares, though the Nikkei's gains were tempered somewhat by caution ahead of ASML's earnings later in the day, a reminder that the AI chip rally's durability still depends on confirmation from the sector's key suppliers. This article was written by Eamonn Sheridan at investinglive.com.
